explanation:
GraphQL is a query language for APIs where the client specifies exactly what data it needs. The schema is the contract — it defines all available types, their fields, and how they relate. Unlike REST (multiple endpoints returning fixed shapes), GraphQL has one endpoint that returns exactly what you ask for, eliminating over-fetching and under-fetching.

The type system has scalars (String, Int, Float, Boolean, ID), objects (with fields), enums, input types (for mutations), and interfaces/unions (for polymorphism). Every field has a type, and the schema is strongly typed — the server validates queries against the schema before executing them.
example:
```graphql
# schema.graphql

# Enum
enum Role {
  ADMIN
  USER
  GUEST
}

# Object type
type User {
  id: ID!
  name: String!
  email: String!
  role: Role!
  posts: [Post!]!
  createdAt: String!
}

type Post {
  id: ID!
  title: String!
  content: String!
  author: User!
  tags: [String!]!
  published: Boolean!
  comments: [Comment!]!
}

type Comment {
  id: ID!
  text: String!
  author: User!
  createdAt: String!
}

# Input types for mutations
input CreateUserInput {
  name: String!
  email: String!
  role: Role = USER
}

input UpdatePostInput {
  title: String
  content: String
  tags: [String!]
  published: Boolean
}

# Pagination
input PaginationInput {
  page: Int = 1
  limit: Int = 10
}

type PaginatedPosts {
  posts: [Post!]!
  total: Int!
  page: Int!
  totalPages: Int!
  hasNext: Boolean!
}

# Root types
type Query {
  user(id: ID!): User
  users(role: Role, pagination: PaginationInput): [User!]!
  post(id: ID!): Post
  posts(tag: String, pagination: PaginationInput): PaginatedPosts!
  me: User
}

type Mutation {
  createUser(input: CreateUserInput!): User!
  updateUser(id: ID!, input: UpdatePostInput!): User!
  deleteUser(id: ID!): Boolean!
  createPost(title: String!, content: String!): Post!
  updatePost(id: ID!, input: UpdatePostInput!): Post!
  addComment(postId: ID!, text: String!): Comment!
}

type Subscription {
  postCreated: Post!
  commentAdded(postId: ID!): Comment!
}
```
key_takeaway: The schema is the API contract — define types, their fields, and relationships. Input types for mutations, enums for fixed values, and `!` for required fields.
try_it: Extend the schema with a `Like` type that connects users to posts. Add `likePost(postId: ID!)` and `unlikePost(postId: ID!)` mutations, and a `likeCount` field on `Post`.

explanation:
Queries read data — they're the GraphQL equivalent of GET requests. The client specifies exactly which fields to return, including nested relations. The server resolves each field independently, so asking for `user.posts.comments` in one query replaces three REST calls. This is GraphQL's superpower: one request, exactly the data you need.

Mutations write data — they're the equivalent of POST/PUT/DELETE. They follow the same syntax as queries but execute sequentially (queries execute in parallel). Variables use `$` syntax and are declared with types. Fragments define reusable field sets to avoid repetition across queries.
key_takeaway: Queries read data (parallel execution), mutations write data (sequential execution). Fragments define reusable field sets. Variables use `$name: Type` syntax and are passed as JSON.
try_it: Write a query that fetches a user, their last 5 posts, and the comment count for each post. Use fragments for the post fields and variables for the user ID and limit.

explanation:
Resolvers are functions that populate each field in a GraphQL schema. When a query arrives, the server walks the query tree and calls the resolver for each field. The resolver receives four arguments: `parent` (the resolved parent object), `args` (field arguments), `context` (shared state like auth and database), and `info` (query metadata).

The resolver chain mirrors the query structure: the root resolver fetches the user, the `posts` resolver on User fetches that user's posts, and the `comments` resolver on Post fetches comments. Each resolver only needs to know how to get its own data — the framework handles the composition.
key_takeaway: Resolvers map schema fields to data-fetching functions. The `parent` object flows down the tree — `User.posts` receives the User, `Post.author` receives the Post. Context carries auth and database.
try_it: Implement a resolver for a `searchUsers(query: String!)` field that searches by name and email with case-insensitive matching. Add pagination and return a `PaginatedUsers` type.

explanation:
Subscriptions enable real-time data by maintaining a persistent connection between client and server. When a mutation creates new data (a comment, a message), it publishes an event. Subscribed clients receive the event instantly via WebSocket. This powers live chat, notifications, collaborative editing, and dashboards.

Subscriptions use the publish-subscribe pattern: mutations `publish` events to channels, and subscriptions `subscribe` to channels. The server typically uses WebSockets (via `graphql-ws` or `subscriptions-transport-ws`). Each subscription creates a long-lived connection that stays open until the client disconnects.
key_takeaway: Subscriptions use WebSockets for real-time data. Mutations `publish` events to channels; subscriptions `subscribe` to receive them. Use `asyncIterator` with channel names for event routing.
try_it: Implement a `typingIndicator` subscription that broadcasts when a user starts typing in a channel. Include the user's name and channel ID. Auto-expire after 3 seconds.

explanation:
GraphQL clients handle caching, optimistic updates, and local state management. Apollo Client is the most popular — it normalizes query results into a cache, so updating one query automatically updates all queries that reference the same entity. URQL is a lighter alternative with a smaller bundle size.

Apollo's cache uses `__typename` and `id` to normalize objects. When you mutate a post, any query that fetched that post updates automatically. Optimistic updates show the change immediately (before the server responds) for instant UI feedback. Refetch queries ensure the cache stays consistent after mutations that create or delete data.
key_takeaway: Apollo Client normalizes cache by `__typename:id`. Mutations update the cache automatically. Use `optimisticResponse` for instant UI feedback and `cache.modify` for manual cache updates.
try_it: Implement infinite scrolling for the posts list using `fetchMore` with cache merging. Add optimistic updates for the `addComment` mutation that immediately shows the comment before server confirmation.

explanation:
The N+1 problem is GraphQL's biggest performance pitfall. When you query 50 users with their posts, naive resolvers make 1 query for users + 50 queries for each user's posts = 51 database calls. DataLoader solves this by batching and caching: it collects all requested IDs within a single tick, makes one batched query, and distributes results back.

DataLoader uses a batch function that receives an array of keys and returns an array of results (in the same order). It caches results per request, so the same user requested by multiple fields only loads once. Each request gets a new DataLoader instance to prevent stale data across requests.
key_takeaway: DataLoader batches N+1 queries into a single query per level. Create new loaders per request to prevent stale data. Use `.load(id)` in resolvers — DataLoader handles batching automatically.
try_it: Implement a `postsByTagLoader` that batches tag-based post lookups. Given 10 posts each with 3 tags, verify it makes 1 query instead of 30.

explanation:
GraphQL interview problems focus on schema design, resolver optimization, and the N+1 query problem. The key patterns are: designing types that map cleanly to your domain, using DataLoader to batch and cache database queries, implementing pagination with cursors, and handling authorization at the resolver level.

Unlike REST where the server controls response shape, GraphQL gives that power to the client. This creates unique challenges: query complexity limits, depth limiting, and rate limiting per field rather than per endpoint. Practice writing schemas that are flexible yet safe.
key_takeaway: GraphQL schemas define the contract; resolvers fetch the data. DataLoader is essential to prevent N+1 queries — batch and cache every relationship lookup.
try_it: Design a schema for a blog platform with Users, Posts, and Comments. Implement a resolver that uses DataLoader to avoid N+1 queries when fetching post authors.

practice_challenges.title: Design a Schema for an E-Commerce API
practice_challenges.hint: Think about entities (Product, Category, Order, User) and their relationships. Use input types for mutations.
practice_challenges.description: Design a complete GraphQL schema for an e-commerce platform with products, categories, shopping carts, and orders.
practice_challenges.example_input: Requirements: browse products by category, add to cart, place order
practice_challenges.example_output: Schema with Query, Mutation, and Subscription types covering all entities

practice_challenges.title: Implement Cursor-Based Pagination
practice_challenges.hint: Use the Relay connection spec: edges, node, cursor, and pageInfo with hasNextPage/hasPreviousPage.
practice_challenges.description: Implement cursor-based pagination for a posts query following the Relay connection specification.
practice_challenges.example_output: Connection with edges, nodes, cursors, and pageInfo

practice_challenges.title: Batch Queries with DataLoader
practice_challenges.hint: Create a DataLoader per request context. The batch function receives an array of keys and must return results in the same order.
practice_challenges.description: Given a resolver that fetches user posts and each post needs the author, use DataLoader to batch all author lookups into a single database query.
practice_challenges.example_output: 1 batched query instead of 50 individual queries

practice_challenges.title: Query Complexity and Depth Limiting
practice_challenges.hint: Assign a complexity cost to each field. Reject queries exceeding a threshold. Limit nesting depth to prevent circular query attacks.
practice_challenges.description: Implement query complexity analysis that rejects queries exceeding 1000 complexity points and depth exceeding 10 levels.
practice_challenges.example_input: Deeply nested query: users { posts { comments { author { posts } } } }
practice_challenges.example_output: Query rejected: depth 5 exceeds limit of 4

practice_challenges.title: Real-Time Subscriptions with WebSocket
practice_challenges.hint: Use GraphQL subscriptions with a PubSub engine. Each subscription listens for specific events and pushes updates to connected clients.
practice_challenges.description: Implement a real-time chat subscription that pushes new messages to all connected clients in a specific chat room.
practice_challenges.example_output: Real-time message delivery via WebSocket to all subscribers in room-1
